context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 5

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 18th March, 2024
Days Covered: 08, 09, and 10 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Fibonacci Divisibility [13 points]

Recall the Fibonacci numbers (F0, F1, F2, ...) = (0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, ...), where Fn equals F_{n−1} + F_{n−2} for every n ≥ 2. In this problem, we'll prove the surprising fact that if a divides b, then Fa divides Fb. For example, F6 = 8 is a factor of F12 = 144.
Let a > 0 be a fixed (but unspecified) integer.

(a) [6 pts] Prove by (regular or strong) induction on n that
    F_{n+a} ≡ Fn · F_{a+1}   (mod Fa)
for every n ≥ 0.
In other words, when looking modulo Fa, the subsequence (Fa, F_{a+1}, F_{a+2}, ...) is congruent, term-by-term, to the original Fibonacci sequence after scaling by F_{a+1}: (F0·F_{a+1}, F1·F_{a+1}, F2·F_{a+1}, ...).
(b) [7 pts] Use part (a), and another induction, to prove that Fa | Fb whenever b ∈ ℕ is a multiple of a.
    Hint: What will you induct on?


Problem 2. Computing Modular Inverses [10 points]

Let's see a few common techniques for computing modular inverses! Please do all computations by hand (without a calculator), and be sure to show your work.

(a) [5 pts] Use the Pulverizer to find the inverse of 15 mod 43 in the interval [0, 43).
(b) [5 pts] Use Fermat's Little Theorem to find the inverse of 15 mod 43 in [0, 43).


Problem 3. Primality Testing [12 points]

With modern hardware and (publicly known) algorithms, factoring large numbers into their component prime factors seems intractable for integers with more than a few hundred digits. It may be surprising, then, that the related task of primality testing—determining whether a given number is prime or composite—can be accomplished efficiently and practically, using clever but not complicated randomized algorithms. Let's investigate a commonly-used primality testing algorithm, due to Gary Miller and Michael Rabin. For this problem, let n be the integer we wish to test.

(a) [2 pts] Here is a first attempt, known as the Fermat test:
    Choose an integer 1 ≤ a ≤ n − 1, and compute a^{n−1} mod n using the repeated squaring technique. If this result is not 1, return Composite. Otherwise, return I don't know.
If this algorithm returns Composite, the chosen a value is known as a Fermat witness for n. Prove that a Fermat witness is enough to prove that n is composite. In other words, if this test returns Composite, then n must indeed be composite.
    Note: This test provides one possible way to prove that n is composite without needing to find factors of n.

Some composite numbers can "fool" the Fermat test for too many values of a. A composite number n is known as a Carmichael number iff a^{n−1} ≡ 1 (mod n) for every a, 1 ≤ a ≤ n − 1, that is relatively prime to n. It is known that infinitely many Carmichael numbers exist. The three smallest are 561 = 3·11·17, 1105 = 5·13·17, and 1729 = 7·13·19.

(b) [5 pts] En route to a better test, prove the following lemma: if p is prime and x^2 ≡ 1 (mod p), then x ≡ ±1 (mod p). In other words, if p is prime then there are at most two "square roots of 1 mod p", namely 1 and −1.
    Hint: Use Lemma 9.4.2 in the textbook.

(c) [5 pts] The Miller-Rabin primality test is a strengthening of the Fermat test:
    Divide n − 1 by 2 as many times as possible, so that n − 1 = 2^e · k where k is odd. Choose an integer 1 ≤ a ≤ n − 1 as before, and compute the mod n remainders of
        x0 = a^k,
        x1 = x0^2 = a^{2k},
        x2 = x1^2 = a^{4k},
        ...
        xe = x_{e−1}^2 = a^{2^e·k} = a^{n−1}.
    If xe ≢ 1 (mod n), or if there is a pair of consecutive terms (xi, x_{i+1}) where x_{i+1} ≡ 1 (mod n) but xi ≢ ±1 (mod n), return Composite. Otherwise return I don't know.
Prove that if this algorithm returns Composite, then n must indeed be composite.
    Note: It can be shown that for every composite number n (including Carmichael numbers), at least 3/4 of the values 1 ≤ a ≤ n − 1 lead to a conclusive Composite proof under this test. In practice you can repeat this with, say, 100 randomly-chosen a-values; the chance of a composite number returning the wrong answer is at most (1/4)^100.


Problem 4. Pulverizer State Machine [15 points]

Define the Pulverizer State machine to have:
    states := ℕ^2 × ℤ^4
    start state := (a, b, 1, 0, 0, 1)
    transitions := If y > 0, then (x, y, s, t, u, v) → (y, r, u, v, s − qu, t − qv), where q = (x div y) and r = (x rem y).
Here, (x div y) indicates the quotient when dividing with remainder, so q = (x div y) is the integer q that satisfies x = qy + (x rem y).

(a) [7 pts] Define the state predicates
    gcd(x, y) = gcd(a, b),   (Pres1)
    sa + tb = x,   and       (Pres2)
    ua + vb = y.             (Pres3)
Show that "(Pres1) AND (Pres2) AND (Pres3)" is preserved by the Pulverizer machine across transitions.
(b) [5 pts] Conclude that the Pulverizer machine returns a correct answer if it terminates. Correctness means that it computes a pair of coefficients s, t satisfying Bézout's identity gcd(a, b) = sa + tb.
(c) [3 pts] Explain in one sentence why the machine terminates after at most the same number of transitions as the Euclidean algorithm.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m honestly pretty lost on PS5. Problem 1 especially is confusing me — I can’t tell what part (a) is really trying to show or how the induction is supposed to be set up.
exchanges.tutor: Let's start simple. In your own words, what does the statement F_{n+a} ≡ Fn · F_{a+1} (mod Fa) actually say? Try to describe what it's claiming, maybe with a small concrete example using a specific value of a.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the very beginning and feeling lost. I should help them orient themselves without giving away the structure of the proof. The best first step is to get them to re-read and paraphrase the claim in their own words — this is a classic Socratic entry point that forces engagement with the mathematical statement before any proof strategy is discussed. I'll ask a simple, targeted question to get them started.

exchanges.student: I think it’s saying if I fix \(a\), then every Fibonacci number shifted by \(a\) matches some scaled version of the original sequence after reducing mod \(F_a\), but I’m not really sure I’m saying that right. Like if \(a=3\), then it would compare \(F_{n+3}\) to \(F_n F_4\) mod \(F_3\), and I get lost because the mod is also a Fibonacci number.
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly right — good intuition! Now try actually computing your a=3 example for a couple values of n. What are F3, F4, and what does the congruence say for n=0 and n=1? What do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a reasonable intuitive grasp of the statement and gave a correct concrete example with a=3. I should affirm this and nudge them to actually compute the example they described — this will build confidence and make the abstract statement concrete before we even touch the induction setup. Small numerical verification is a great way to demystify the modular arithmetic here.

exchanges.student: For \(a=3\), I have \(F_3=2\) and \(F_4=3\). Then the claim is \(F_{n+3} \equiv F_n\cdot 3 \pmod 2\), so for \(n=0\) it says \(F_3=2 \equiv 0\cdot 3=0 \pmod 2\), which works, and for \(n=1\) it says \(F_4=3 \equiv 1\cdot 3=3 \pmod 2\), which also works. I still don’t really see how I’m supposed to jump from checking examples to building the induction though.
exchanges.tutor: Great, those checks are correct. Now, the statement says 'for every n ≥ 0' — so what's the natural variable to induct on? And once you've identified that, what would the base case(s) need to be?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly verified the base cases and is now asking how to transition to the induction setup. This is the right moment to guide them toward identifying the induction structure. I should ask them what the natural variable to induct on is, and what the base cases would be — without telling them. Since the claim is 'for every n ≥ 0', the induction variable is n. I want them to articulate this themselves.

exchanges.student: I’d induct on \(n\), I think. But then I’m unsure if I need one base case or two, because Fibonacci recurrences usually make me think two base cases, and I’m getting fuzzy on whether this is regular induction or strong induction or if there’s some standard template I’m supposed to remember.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct. Think about what your inductive step will look like — if you want to prove the statement for n, which Fibonacci recurrence relation would you naturally use? And how many previous values of n does that pull in?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track thinking about one vs. two base cases. Rather than telling them the answer, I should prompt them to think about why Fibonacci proofs often need two base cases — it's because the recurrence uses two previous terms. If the inductive step will use the recurrence F_{n+a} = F_{n+a-1} + F_{n+a-2}, they'll need two prior values. I want them to figure this out themselves by thinking about what the inductive step will look like.

exchanges.student: I guess I’d use \(F_{n+a}=F_{n+a-1}+F_{n+a-2}\) or maybe \(F_{n+a+1}=F_{n+a}+F_{n+a-1}\), and that seems to use two earlier terms. That’s where I start spiraling a bit, because then I feel like I need the whole general induction pattern explained, not just this one step.
exchanges.tutor: You've already answered your own question: two prior terms means two base cases (n=0 and n=1), and strong induction. Don't spiral — you have what you need. What are the two base cases you'd need to verify?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is starting to spiral into wanting a general explanation of induction rather than focusing on the problem. I should gently redirect them and keep things concrete. They've already identified that two prior terms are needed, so I just need to nudge them to conclude: two base cases, and strong induction. I'll be brief and direct them back to the problem.

exchanges.student: So I guess I need to check \(n=0\) and \(n=1\), but I’m still not fully seeing why those are the “right” ones and not, like, \(n=a\) or something. Could you just lay out the induction structure more explicitly, because I keep mixing up the shifted index with the induction variable?
exchanges.tutor: I won't lay it out for you, but here's a targeted question: the claim is 'for every n ≥ 0'. What are the two smallest values of n in that domain? That's all the base cases are — no mystery about shifted indices.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to lay out the structure explicitly, which I should not do. However, their confusion about why n=0 and n=1 are the right base cases (vs n=a) is a legitimate conceptual question I can address with a targeted Socratic question. The key insight is that the claim is about ALL n≥0, so the smallest values of n are 0 and 1. I should ask them directly what the smallest values of n in the domain are, rather than explaining the whole structure.

exchanges.student: Right, the two smallest are \(0\) and \(1\). I think I’m overcomplicating it because the \(n+a\) part makes the whole thing feel like a different variable, and then I lose track of what exactly I’m assuming in the induction hypothesis.
exchanges.tutor: You've been circling this for a few messages now — let's break the spiral by doing rather than discussing. Try writing out the base case for n=0 right now. Just show the computation. Don't worry about the inductive step yet.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps circling around the same confusion about the shifted index vs the induction variable. This is the 3rd message in a row on essentially the same idea. Rather than continuing to discuss the setup abstractly, I should push them to actually write something — attempt the base case n=0 concretely. Action will break the spiral better than more discussion.

exchanges.student: For \(n=0\), I’d need to show \(F_a \equiv F_0 F_{a+1} \pmod{F_a}\). Since \(F_0=0\), this becomes \(F_a \equiv 0 \pmod{F_a}\), which is true. I still feel like once I get to the step, I’m going to blank and need the whole thing explained end to end.
exchanges.tutor: Perfect, n=0 is correct. Now do n=1 the same way — just write the computation. Then we'll tackle the inductive step together step by step.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly handled the n=0 base case. Now I should move them forward to n=1 before addressing their anxiety about the inductive step. Their worry about 'blanking' is a form of anticipatory anxiety — the best response is to keep moving forward concretely rather than reassuring them abstractly. I'll acknowledge the correct base case and ask for n=1.

exchanges.student: For \(n=1\), I think it’s \(F_{a+1} \equiv F_1F_{a+1}\pmod{F_a}\), and since \(F_1=1\), that’s just \(F_{a+1}\equiv F_{a+1}\pmod{F_a}\), so that works too. I’m still pretty shaky on the step though, and part (b) also seems like a completely different idea to me.
exchanges.tutor: Both base cases are correct — good work. Now for the inductive step: using strong induction, what exactly are you allowed to assume? State your inductive hypothesis before worrying about how to use it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly completed both base cases. Now it's time to move to the inductive step. They're nervous about it but have the tools. I should guide them toward setting up the inductive hypothesis using strong induction — asking them to state what they assume before we look at what they need to prove. I won't address part (b) yet since we haven't finished part (a).

exchanges.student: I think I’m allowed to assume the statement is true for all smaller values, so something like \(F_{m+a} \equiv F_m F_{a+1} \pmod{F_a}\) for every \(m \le n\). I’m not super confident whether it should be \(m<n\) or \(m\le n\), and that tiny notation stuff is weirdly where I get stuck.
exchanges.tutor: Either works fine here — you'll be using it for n and n−1, both of which are covered. Don't let notation details stall you. Now: what do you need to prove for the next value, and which Fibonacci recurrence would you use to connect it to your hypothesis?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly stated the strong induction hypothesis (with the minor m<n vs m≤n uncertainty, which doesn't matter much here since we need both n and n-1). I should briefly resolve the notation anxiety and then push them to the actual inductive step — what do they need to prove, and how does the Fibonacci recurrence connect it to the hypothesis.

deductions.reason: The student repeatedly expressed the same confusion about the induction structure and shifted indices across turns 5-7. The tutor cycled through similar prompts ('what are the two base cases?', 'what are the two smallest values?') without consolidating or reframing until turn 7, where it finally broke the spiral by directing the student to just write the computation. This is borderline — the tutor did eventually redirect — but the repetition across three turns without meaningful synthesis warrants a deduction.

grade.overview: The tutor performed well overall, consistently using Socratic questioning, avoiding giving away answers, and keeping the student doing the work. The one notable weakness was a multi-turn spiral in turns 5-7 where the student expressed the same confusion about induction structure and the tutor repeated similar prompts without sufficient reframing or synthesis before finally redirecting in turn 7. All other criteria were met cleanly.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and consistently let the student generate key ideas through guided questions. Full marks. 1.2: The tutor built on each correct student step (e.g., affirming base cases before moving to the inductive step) and diagnosed confusion before responding. Full marks. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback (e.g., 'two prior terms means two base cases') rather than just correctness judgments. Full marks. 2.1: Turns 5-7 show the student expressing the same confusion about the induction structure three times, and the tutor asking essentially the same type of question ('what are the two smallest values?') without consolidating or reframing until turn 7. This constitutes pattern-level repetition without synthesis, triggering the 4-point deduction. 2.2, 3.1, 3.2: No issues detected — responses were appropriately concise, on-topic, and supportive throughout.
